prompt:
ܦܪܩܐ ܐ

ܐ:ܐ ܘܟܕ ܢܳܚܶܬ ܨܰܦܪܳܐ ܘܢܽܘܗܪܳܐ ܕܫܶܡܫܳܐ ܡܶܬܓܠܶܐ ܥܰܠ ܐܰܪܥܳܐ܆ ܢܩܽܘܡ ܒܰܪܢܳܫܳܐ ܡܶܢ ܡܰܫܟܒܶܗ ܘܢܶܬܬܥܺܝܪ ܡܶܢ ܫܶܢܬܶܗ ܒܛܰܝܒܽܘܬܳܐ ܘܰܒܬܰܘܕܺܝܬܳܐ ܠܡܳܪܶܐ ܟܽܠܳܐ܀
(And when the morning descends and the light of the sun is revealed upon the earth, let a man arise from his bed and be awakened from his sleep, in grace and in thanksgiving to the Lord of all.)

ܐ:ܒ ܘܢܰܫܺܝܓ ܐܰܦܰܘ̈ܗܝ ܒܡܰܝ̈ܳܐ ܕܰܟ̈ܝܳܐ ܘܢܰܕܟܶܐ ܦܰܓܪܶܗ ܡܶܢ ܟܽܠ ܛܰܡܽܐܘܬܳܐ ܕܠܺܠܝܳܐ܆ ܐܰܝܟ ܕܡܶܬܕܰܟܶܐ ܢܰܦܫܳܐ ܒܰܨܠܽܘܬܳܐ ܕܨܰܦܪܳܐ܀
(And let him wash his face in pure waters and cleanse his body from all uncleanness of the night, even as the soul is cleansed by the prayer of the morning.)

ܐ:ܓ ܘܰܢܫܰܪܶܐ ܝܰܘܡܶܗ ܒܫܶܬܩܳܐ ܘܰܒܪܶܢܝܳܐ ܘܰܒܗܶܪܓܳܐ ܕܠܶܒܳܐ܆ ܡܶܛܽܠ ܕܗܰܘ ܕܰܡܫܰܪܶܐ ܨܰܦܪܶܗ ܒܰܡܢܳܬ ܫܰܝܢܳܐ ܒܳܢܶܐ ܫܶܬܶܐܣܬܳܐ ܕܝܰܘܡܳܐ ܟܽܠܶܗ ܥܰܠ ܫܽܘܥܳܐ ܬܰܩܺܝܦܳܐ܀
(And let him begin his day in silence and in meditation and in pondering of the heart, for he who begins his morning in a portion of peace builds the foundation of the whole day upon a mighty rock.)

ܐ:ܕ ܘܟܶܢ ܢܶܐܟܽܘܠ ܠܰܚܡܳܐ ܕܨܰܦܪܳܐ ܠܚܰܝܠܳܐ ܕܦܰܓܪܶܗ ܘܰܠܣܽܘܝܳܟܳܐ ܕܢܰܦܫܶܗ܆ ܡܶܛܽܠ ܕܰܟܬܺܝܒ ܕܠܳܐ ܒܠܰܚܡܳܐ ܒܰܠܚܽܘܕ ܚܳܝܶܐ ܒܰܪܢܳܫܳܐ ܐܶܠܳܐ ܐܳܦ ܦܰܓܪܳܐ ܣܢܺܝܩ ܥܰܠ ܡܶܐܟܽܘܠܬܳܐ ܕܬܶܗܘܶܐ ܠܶܗ ܚܰܝܠܳܐ ܠܰܥܒܳܕ̈ܰܘܗܝ܀
(And then let him eat the bread of the morning for the strength of his body and for the sustenance of his soul, for it is written that not by bread alone does a man live, yet also the body has need of food that there may be strength for his works.)

ܐ:ܗ ܘܢܶܬܩܶܢ ܐܽܘܪ̈ܚܳܬܶܗ ܘܢܰܬܩܶܢ ܥܒ̈ܳܕܰܘܗܝ ܩܕܳܡ ܕܢܶܦܽܘܩ ܡܶܢ ܒܰܝܬܶܗ ܠܰܫܘ̈ܩܶܐ ܘܰܠܚܰܩ̈ܠܳܬܳܐ܆ ܡܶܛܽܠ ܕܗܰܘ ܕܡܰܬܩܶܢ ܥܒ̈ܳܕܰܘܗܝ ܡܶܢ ܩܕܺܝܡ ܡܳܪܝܳܐ ܡܰܫܰܪ ܦܽܘܠܚܳܢܶܗ܀
(And let him set in order his ways and prepare his works before he goes forth from his house to the streets and to the fields, for he who prepares his works from beforehand, the Lord prospers his labor.)

ܐ:ܘ ܘܢܶܬܕܟܰܪ ܒܰܪܢܳܫܳܐ ܕܟܽܠ ܨܰܦܪܳܐ ܡܰܘܗܰܒܬܳܐ ܗ̱ܝ ܡܶܢ ܝܰܕ ܡܳܪܝܳܐ ܐܰܠܳܗܳܐ ܘܠܳܐ ܬܶܗܘܶܐ ܠܶܗ ܠܰܨܪܺܝܟܽܘܬܳܐ ܐܶܠܳܐ ܠܒܽܘܪܟܬܳܐ ܘܰܠܣܰܒܪܳܐ܀
(And let a man remember that every morning is a gift from the hand of the Lord God, and let it not be unto him for vexation but rather for blessing and for hope.)
response:
# ܦܪܩܐ ܐ — ܢܘܗܪܐ ܘܦܘܫܩܐ

## ܡܰܒܘܥܳܐ ܘܛܶܟܣܳܐ ܕܟܬܳܒܳܐ

This passage, titled **ܦܪܩܐ ܐ** (Chapter One), presents itself as a wisdom text structured in the manner of classical Syriac didactic literature. It is composed of six verses (ܐ:ܐ through ܐ:ܘ) and follows the formal, rhythmic prose style characteristic of Syriac patristic and monastic instructional writings. The text prescribes a morning rule of life — a spiritual and practical discipline for beginning each day.

---

## ܣܘܟܳܠܳܐ ܕܟܠ ܦܬܓܡܐ (Analysis of Each Verse)

### ܐ:ܐ — The Awakening

The chapter opens with the image of dawn as a cosmic event: **ܢܳܚܶܬ ܨܰܦܪܳܐ** ("the morning descends") and **ܢܽܘܗܪܳܐ ܕܫܶܡܫܳܐ ܡܶܬܓܠܶܐ ܥܰܠ ܐܰܪܥܳܐ** ("the light of the sun is revealed upon the earth"). The verb ܡܶܬܓܠܶܐ (is revealed/manifested) carries theological weight in Syriac — it is the same root (ܓ-ܠ-ܐ) used for divine revelation. Thus the sunrise is implicitly a theophany, a daily unveiling of God's creative act.

The human response is to **arise** (ܢܩܽܘܡ) and **be awakened** (ܢܶܬܬܥܺܝܪ) — two distinct acts. The first is physical, the second spiritual. The pairing of **ܛܰܝܒܽܘܬܳܐ** (grace/gratitude) and **ܬܰܘܕܺܝܬܳܐ** (thanksgiving/confession of praise) establishes the theological frame: the day begins not with human initiative but with receptivity to divine gift.

### ܐ:ܒ — The Washing

The second verse introduces ritual purification: **ܢܰܫܺܝܓ ܐܰܦܰܘ̈ܗܝ ܒܡܰܝ̈ܳܐ ܕܰܟ̈ܝܳܐ** ("let him wash his face in pure waters"). The act is physical, but the verse immediately draws an analogy: **ܐܰܝܟ ܕܡܶܬܕܰܟܶܐ ܢܰܦܫܳܐ ܒܰܨܠܽܘܬܳܐ ܕܨܰܦܪܳܐ** ("even as the soul is cleansed by the prayer of the morning").

This parallelism between bodily washing and spiritual prayer reflects a deeply Syriac anthropology in which body and soul are not opposed but are analogical mirrors of one another. The word **ܛܰܡܽܐܘܬܳܐ** (uncleanness/impurity) of the night likely refers both to physical uncleanliness and to the spiritual vulnerability associated with sleep — a theme found in the writings of Ephrem and in the Maʿnīyāthā of the night offices.

### ܐ:ܓ — The Silence

This verse is the contemplative heart of the chapter. Three terms define the proper beginning of the day:

- **ܫܶܬܩܳܐ** — silence
- **ܪܶܢܝܳܐ** — meditation, reflection
- **ܗܶܪܓܳܐ ܕܠܶܒܳܐ** — pondering/murmuring of the heart

The word **ܗܶܪܓܳܐ** is particularly rich. It derives from the root ܗ-ܪ-ܓ, which in Syriac means to meditate, to murmur, to recite quietly — cognate with Hebrew הגה as in Psalm 1:2 ("and on his Torah he meditates day and night"). It suggests not empty silence but an interior recitation, a quiet turning of the heart toward wisdom.

The architectural metaphor that follows is striking: **ܒܳܢܶܐ ܫܶܬܶܐܣܬܳܐ ܕܝܰܘܡܳܐ ܟܽܠܶܗ ܥܰܠ ܫܽܘܥܳܐ ܬܰܩܺܝܦܳܐ** ("builds the foundation of the whole day upon a mighty rock"). This echoes the Gospel parable of the wise builder (Matthew 7:24–25), transposed into a daily key: each morning is a new construction, and peace (ܫܰܝܢܳܐ) is the bedrock.

### ܐ:ܕ — The Bread

The fourth verse turns to the body's need: **ܢܶܐܟܽܘܠ ܠܰܚܡܳܐ ܕܨܰܦܪܳܐ** ("let him eat the bread of the morning"). The text is careful to honor the body without idolizing it. It cites the scriptural principle that "not by bread alone does a man live" (cf. Deuteronomy 8:3; Matthew 4:4), but then — in a notable move — it completes the thought not with "but by every word of God" but rather with an affirmation that **the body genuinely needs food** for the strength to do its works.

This is not a contradiction of Scripture but a complementary reading: the verse has already established prayer and meditation (verses ܐ:ܒ–ܓ) as the soul's nourishment. Now it insists that the body too must be fed. The word **ܣܽܘܝܳܟܳܐ** (sustenance, support) applied to the soul suggests that even the act of eating, done rightly, sustains the whole person.

### ܐ:ܗ — The Preparation

Verse five is practical and proverbial in tone: **ܘܢܶܬܩܶܢ ܐܽܘܪ̈ܚܳܬܶܗ ܘܢܰܬܩܶܢ ܥܒ̈ܳܕܰܘܗܝ** ("let him set in order his ways and prepare his works"). The repetition of the root ܬ-ܩ-ܢ (to set right, to order, to prepare) emphasizes deliberateness. The Syriac wisdom tradition consistently values **ܬܽܘܩܳܢܳܐ** — right ordering, preparation, readiness — as a moral and spiritual virtue.

The verse names two destinations: **ܫܘ̈ܩܶܐ** (streets, marketplaces) and **ܚܰܩ̈ܠܳܬܳܐ** (fields). This encompasses the full range of human labor — commercial and agricultural, urban and rural. The closing promise, **ܡܳܪܝܳܐ ܡܰܫܰܪ ܦܽܘܠܚܳܢܶܗ** ("the Lord prospers his labor"), echoes Psalm 90:17 and Proverbs 16:3.

### ܐ:ܘ
